title: Psalms 39
subtitle: Show Me How Fleeting My Life Is

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — “I Am a Stranger Before You”

heb.paragraph: "Each person’s life is but a breath (hebel)" (v.5). Ecclesiastes’ signature word appears in the Psalter. David tried to keep silent but his anguish grew until he spoke (vv.1–3). His request: "Show me, LORD, my life’s end and the number of my days; let me know how fleeting my life is" (v.4). The response: a handbreadth. A mere breath. Hebel.

refs.note: Moses' prayer shares the same meditation on human transience: 'You turn people back to dust … our days may come to seventy years.' Both psalms measure human life against God's eternity and find it vanishingly brief.
James echoes v. 5: 'What is your life? You are a mist that appears for a little while and then vanishes.' The psalm's metaphor of breath (hebel) becomes James' image of vapour.
The repeated term hebel (breath, vapour) connects this psalm to Ecclesiastes' governing metaphor. Both texts use the same word to express the evanescence of human existence.

notes.note: MacArthur: David's attempt to remain silent before the wicked illustrates the tension between godly restraint (Prov 10:19) and the believer's need to pour out his heart to God. Restraint toward men should not become silence toward God.
MacArthur: the prayer for awareness of life's brevity (cf. Ps 90:12, James 4:14) is a recurring biblical theme. Understanding our mortality produces humility, urgency, and proper dependence on God.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'I will put a muzzle on my mouth' (maḥsôm) — the image is violent. The psalmist restrains his speech not gently but forcefully, as one muzzles an animal. The urge to speak is almost uncontrollable.

Alter: 'each man's life is but a breath' (hebel) — the same word Ecclesiastes uses sixty times. Here as there, it means vapour, exhalation — something visible for a moment, then nothing. Human existence at its most solid is still mist.

Calvin: the resolve to be silent fails — the fire burns and the words break out. Calvin: this is honest self-reporting. The psalmist tried to suppress his complaint and could not. Repression does not work; only honest prayer does.
Calvin: 'show me, Lord, my life's end and the number of my days' — Calvin reads this not as morbid curiosity but as a prayer for perspective. Knowledge of brevity is the beginning of wisdom.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'while in the presence of the wicked' — the psalmist's silence is strategic, not universal. He restrains complaint specifically to avoid giving the wicked ammunition. His suffering should not become their entertainment.

NET: hebel ('breath, vapour') carries connotations of both brevity and insubstantiality. Life is not merely short but lightweight — lacking the permanence and significance humans desperately wish it had.

Kidner: the failed silence is psychologically acute. Bottled-up grief does not disappear; it intensifies. The fire metaphor is exact: suppressed emotion heats up until it erupts. Better to speak to God than to explode before the wicked.

Kidner: 'a mere phantom' (ṣelem) — humanity as shadow, image without substance. The word echoes Genesis 1:26-27 (made in God's ṣelem) with bitter irony: the image of God is itself an image, a projection, a shadow.

VanGemeren: the prayer for awareness of mortality is a wisdom-psalm motif (cf. Ps 90:12). Knowing one's finitude is not despair but the foundation of dependence on God. Mortality drives the psalmist toward the Eternal.

VanGemeren: the hebel language connects Psalm 39 to Ecclesiastes' theology. But where Ecclesiastes explores hebel philosophically, the psalmist experiences it existentially — in illness, isolation, and divine discipline.

Goldingay: the muzzle (maḥsôm) is a hapax legomenon — used only here in the Hebrew Bible. The rare word emphasises the extreme measure the psalmist takes. This is not ordinary discretion but desperate self-restraint.

Goldingay: 'busy about nothing' — the hebel of human activity means that accumulation and ambition are equally vaporous. The psalmist does not condemn work but questions its ultimate significance when death cancels all accounts.

sections.header: Verses 7–13 — “Look Away from Me Before I Depart”

heb.paragraph: "I dwell with you as a foreigner (gēr), a stranger, as all my ancestors were" (v.12). Life on earth is temporary residence. David is a guest, not an owner. The psalm closes with a remarkable request: "Look away from me, that I may enjoy life again, before I depart and am no more" (v.13). David wants God to relent before death comes.

refs.note: The confession 'I dwell with you as a foreigner, a stranger, as all my ancestors were' (v. 12) echoes Psalm 119:19: 'I am a stranger on earth.' The resident-alien metaphor describes the life of faith itself.
David's prayer at the temple offering uses the same language: 'We are foreigners and strangers in your sight, as were all our ancestors.' The sentiment passes from private psalm to public liturgy.
Peter addresses believers as 'foreigners and exiles' — the psalm's self-description becomes a defining category for the entire Christian community's relationship to the world.

notes.note: MacArthur: after stripping away every earthly confidence, David arrives at the only reliable foundation: 'My hope is in you.' This is the proper conclusion of meditating on life's brevity — not despair but redirected trust.

MacArthur: identifying as a 'stranger and sojourner' reflects the biblical truth that believers are citizens of heaven living temporarily on earth (Phil 3:20, Heb 11:13). This perspective transforms how we handle loss and suffering.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'And now, Lord, what do I look for?' — the pivot of the psalm. Having declared life vapour and activity futile, the psalmist has only one option left: hope in God. Despair about the world becomes dependence on the Eternal.

Alter: 'a stranger…a sojourner' (gēr…tôšāv) — the psalmist claims the most vulnerable social categories as self-descriptions. Before God, we have no permanent address, no citizen's rights. We are guests in God's house.

Calvin: when everything else is declared hebel, only God remains. Calvin: the stripping away of false hopes is painful but necessary. What survives the fire of disillusionment is the only thing worth hoping in.

Calvin: 'Look away from me, that I may enjoy life again' — the most startling request in the Psalter. The psalmist wants God to stop looking at him. Calvin: this is not rejection of God but exhaustion under God's scrutiny.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'you rebuke and discipline anyone for their sin; you consume their wealth like a moth' — the moth image conveys silent, gradual destruction. God's discipline is not always dramatic; sometimes it works like decay, imperceptibly reducing what seemed secure.
NET: 'a stranger…a sojourner, as all my ancestors were' — the language echoes 1 Chronicles 29:15 and Leviticus 25:23. Even in the promised land, Israel held the land as God's guests, not as owners.

Kidner: the turning point. Everything has been declared futile — life, activity, wealth. The only thing left standing is God. Kidner: the psalm strips away every false support until faith stands alone.

Kidner: 'before I depart and am no more' — the psalm ends in shadow. No praise, no resolution, no lifting of the burden. Like Psalm 88, it refuses a tidy ending. Kidner: Scripture validates prayers that end in the dark.

VanGemeren: the stranger-sojourner motif is central to Israel's self-understanding (cf. 1 Pet 2:11). The people of God are always pilgrims — in Egypt, in the wilderness, and even in the land. Home is eschatological, not geographical.

VanGemeren: the unresolved ending, like Psalm 88, resists easy comfort. The canon preserves these dark prayers because they are true to human experience. Not every night ends in morning within the psalm itself.

Goldingay: the question 'what do I look for?' is both existential and practical. Having declared everything hebel, the psalmist must decide where to invest what remains of his hope. The answer — 'my hope is in you' — is the psalm's quiet revolution.

Goldingay: 'Look away from me' — Job 7:19 makes the same request. The sufferer experiences God's attention not as comfort but as pressure. The desire for God's absence is paradoxically an acknowledgment of God's overwhelming reality.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: One wrestling with the brevity and seeming futility of life (vv.1-6,12-13)
ppl.text: David initially resolves silence — 'I will put a muzzle on my mouth while in the presence of the wicked' (v.1) — but his anguish bursts out: 'Show me, LORD, my life's end and the number of my days; let me know how fleeting my life is' (v.4). Life is measured in 'handbreadths' (tefachim, v.5) — the smallest unit of Hebrew measurement. This is Ecclesiastes-level existential honesty within the Psalter, and it ends with a startling request: 'Look away from me, that I may enjoy life again before I depart and am no more' (v.13).

ppl.name: God (Yahweh)
ppl.role: The discipliner whose silence David both requests and dreads (vv.9-11)
ppl.text: David attributes his suffering to God's direct action: 'You have done this' (v.9). God's 'rebuke and discipline' consume human beauty 'like a moth' (v.11) — an image of slow, inexorable destruction. Yet David calls himself 'a foreigner, a stranger' before God (v.12), echoing Abraham's self-description (Gen 23:4) and establishing a theology of pilgrim identity that Hebrews 11:13 later develops.

ppl.name: Jeduthun
ppl.role: Temple music director named in the superscription
ppl.text: The psalm is assigned 'to the director of music, to Jeduthun.' Jeduthun was one of three Levitical music leaders appointed by David (1 Chr 25:1-6), alongside Asaph and Heman. His name appears in Psalms 39, 62, and 77 superscriptions. The connection suggests this deeply personal lament was incorporated into Israel's public worship repertoire — private anguish becoming communal liturgy.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI said, “I will watch my ways and keep my tongue from sin; I will put a muzzle on my mouth while in the presence of the wicked.”ESVI said, \u201cI will guard my ways, that I may not sin with my tongue; I will guard my mouth with a muzzle, so long as the wicked are in my presence.\u201dNIVI was silent; I would not open my mouth, for you are the one who has done this.ESVI am mute; I do not open my mouth, for it is you who have done it.

rows.label: The Stranger — 39:7–13
rows.text: "I am a foreigner with you." "Look away from me before I depart." Temporary residence.

hebtext.note: "Each person’s life is but a breath (hebel)" (v.5). Ecclesiastes’ signature word appears in the Psalter. David tried to keep silent but his anguish grew until he spoke (vv.1–3). His request: "Show me, LORD, my life’s end and the number of my days; let me know how fleeting my life is" (v.4). The response: a handbreadth. A mere breath. Hebel.

"I dwell with you as a foreigner (gēr), a stranger, as all my ancestors were" (v.12). Life on earth is temporary residence. David is a guest, not an owner. The psalm closes with a remarkable request: "Look away from me, that I may enjoy life again, before I depart and am no more" (v.13). David wants God to relent before death comes.

themes.note: Sovereignty: life’s brevity is God’s design. The hebel theology bridges Ecclesiastes and the Psalter. Faith: living as a stranger before God.
